rows.Best Goals: Muscle gain, general fitness, fat loss

rows.Key Lifts: Bench Press, OHP, Incline DB, Lateral Raise, Triceps
Back Squat, Leg Press, Leg Extension, Calf Raise
Weighted Pull-up, Barbell Row, Rear Delt Fly, Biceps
Deadlift or RDL, Hip Thrust, Leg Curl, Calf Raise

rows.Session: Bench Press, OHP, Incline DB, Lateral Raise, Triceps Pushdown
Deadlift, Weighted Pull-up, Barbell Row, Face Pull, Barbell Curl
Back Squat, Leg Press, Leg Curl, Calf Raise
Incline Barbell, DB Shoulder Press, Cable Fly, Overhead Triceps Ext
Rack Pull or Pendlay Row, Lat Pulldown, Cable Row, Rear Delt, Hammer Curl
Front Squat or Hack Squat, RDL, Leg Extension, Seated Calf Raise

rows.Modification: Drop to 1 compound + 1 accessory per movement pattern; supersets to save time; reduce rest to 45–60s.
Full session + extra accessory/isolation work or intensity techniques.
Only for advanced/powerlifting peaking sessions; risk of diminishing returns and excess fatigue for hypertrophy-only goals beyond ~90 min.

rows.Trigger: Training age crosses ~1 year on Full Body
rows.Action: Migrate to Upper/Lower or PPL for more frequency-adjusted volume capacity

rows.Trigger: Weekly sets/muscle exceed what current split can service without >90 min sessions
rows.Action: Add a day or migrate to higher-frequency split

rows.Trigger: Persistent joint pain from a pattern-heavy split (e.g., Upper/Lower with too much pressing)
rows.Action: Rebalance push:pull ratio, or move to PPL for better pattern distribution

rows.Trigger: Life stress/recovery capacity drops (poor sleep <6h, high stress)
rows.Action: Temporarily downshift to fewer days (e.g., 6-day PPL → 4-day Upper/Lower)
Check recovery first, then consider split change as last resort (rule: don't over-attribute plateaus to "wrong split" — see 2_Programming_Rules.txt for full plateau decision tree)

rows.Failure Frequency: Avoid true failure entirely; technique breakdown risk too high
Occasional (last set of an exercise, isolation only)
Regularly on last set of isolation work
Common on isolation, selective on compounds
Programmed failure, forced reps, drop sets as planned tools

rows.Rationale: Full ATP-PC recovery for max force output
Metabolic stress is a feature, not a bug

rows.Failure Policy: Rare, only last set, only with a spotter/safety pins
Occasional, programmed (e.g., last week of a block)
Common, including forced reps and drop sets
Never — autoregulate down regardless of program

rows.Adjustment: Reduce weekly sets by 20–30%, add 1 RIR to targets, prioritize compounds over accessories
Standard programming per tables above
Can push toward MRV, lower RIR more often, tolerate higher frequency
Reduce session intensity, avoid failure entirely, consider trimming volume 10-20% that week
Slight caution; avoid maximal RIR pushes
Standard programming
Full capacity; ideal for pushing progression
Treat like poor sleep — reduce volume/intensity, protect adherence over optimization
Standard, monitor
Full programming capacity

rows.Programming Note: Emphasize technique mastery before intensity; avoid true failure and max singles; allow faster recovery (can often handle slightly higher frequency)
Full programming range applies; highest general work capacity
Slightly longer warm-ups; monitor joint feedback; recovery generally still robust
Add extra warm-up sets, prioritize joint-friendly variations, slightly more conservative failure policy
Prioritize machines/controlled ranges for higher-risk lifts, longer general warm-up (10-15 min), deload more frequently (every 4-6 weeks vs 6-8), emphasize tendon/connective tissue recovery time

rows.Marker: Can they squat to depth with a dowel/empty bar safely?

rows.Notes: Minimum effective dose; fine for true beginners
Gold standard for beginners; allows technique repetition

rows.Split: Full Body A/B/A/B or early Upper/Lower
rows.Notes: Only once movement competency is established (~8-12 weeks in)
Recovery capacity and technique consistency not yet built; risk of overuse and burnout; cap at 4 quality days

rows.Adjustment: Standard linear progression, moderate surplus (~250-500 kcal above maintenance)
Same resistance program; deficit ~300-500 kcal; expect body recomposition (fat loss + some muscle gain) which is uniquely achievable at this training age
2-3x/week full body sufficient; add 2 cardio sessions/week
Still linear progression but bias rep ranges toward 4-6 on main lifts sooner (~week 8+)

rows.Beginner Program Adjustment: Squat, push-up progressions, inverted rows (table/bar), glute bridge, plank
Band squat, band row, band press, band pull-apart, band RDL
Full program capability; covers 90% of beginner needs
Introduce around week 4-10 once technique is coached
DB + bands sufficient for 6-12 months of real progress
Full machine + free weight access; use machines to teach movement patterns safely (e.g., leg press before back squat for a nervous or deconditioned client)

rows.Problem: Client is extremely sore for a week
rows.Fix: Reduce volume 30-50% next session, ensure adequate protein/sleep, reassure that this resolves within 2-4 weeks

rows.Problem: Client can't feel a muscle working (e.g., "I don't feel my back")
rows.Fix: Add lighter, higher-rep isolation work with a slower eccentric to build mind-muscle connection (e.g., band pull-aparts, cable rows) before loading barbell rows heavily
Check recovery/sleep/nutrition first; verify technique isn't limiting load artificially

rows.Problem: Client wants to add advanced techniques (drop sets, etc.) immediately
rows.Fix: Redirect — technique and adherence first; intensity techniques add fatigue without proportional benefit until at least 6 months in

rows.Marker: Linear progression has stalled on most main lifts
rows.Description: Can no longer add weight every session
Client can self-report proximity to failure with ~1 RIR accuracy
Adding a rep or small load weekly/bi-weekly still works with double progression
Benefits from planned variation (DUP, blocks) rather than pure linear add-weight
Can now handle MEV-to-MAV range volumes without excess fatigue

rows.Recommended Split: PPL x2 (6-day) — only once work capacity supports it

rows.Corrective Exercise Addition: Close-grip bench, board press, overhead triceps work
Paused bench, spoto press, incline DB press
Pause squats, front squats, tempo squats
Face pulls, rear delt fly, high rows added 2-3x/week
Increase direct rear delt volume to 6-9 sets/week

rows.Programming Emphasis: Higher volume (upper MEV-MAV range), moderate rep ranges 6-15, isolation-heavy accessories
Lower rep ranges on mains (3-6), moderate accessory volume, DUP or block periodization
Maintain volume near current MEV-MAV to preserve muscle; add cardio/NEAT separately rather than cutting resistance volume first
Moderate volume, protein-forward nutrition (handled in file 7), patience — recomp is slower than dedicated bulk/cut
Blend in power/explosive work 1-2x/week, reduce isolation volume in favor of multi-joint and unilateral work

rows.Problem: Stalled on a lift for 3+ weeks
rows.Fix: Run plateau decision tree (file 2, section 7)

rows.Problem: Chronic joint ache in a specific pattern
rows.Fix: Reduce direct volume on offending pattern 30-50%, add rotator cuff/tendon-friendly accessory work, reassess exercise selection (see file 9)

rows.Problem: Feels like "no progress" despite PRs on isolation lifts
rows.Fix: Reframe expectations — intermediates progress in smaller, less frequent increments; track multiple metrics (volume load, measurements, photos) not just top-set weight
Cross-check against file 10 fatigue indicators before assuming programming is "wrong"

rows.Practical Use: Target range for normal productive blocks
Ceiling — training above this without adjustment leads to overreaching

Used to pick exercises — favor high-SFR movements (e.g., leg curl for hamstrings) over low-SFR ones (e.g., stiff-leg deadlift, which is highly fatiguing per unit of hamstring stimulus) especially late in a block when fatigue is high

rows.Note: Full capacity, may need slightly longer warm-ups
Consider capping true 1RM testing frequency, rely on submaximal RPE-based strength tracking; prioritize joint-friendly variations for MRV-pushing exercises

Longer deload/recovery cycles, greater reliance on machines for MRV-range volume to reduce joint loading, still fully capable of meaningful strength/hypertrophy gains but with more conservative fatigue management

rows.Problem: Chronic joint pain across multiple lifts
rows.Fix: Full deload + exercise substitution audit (file 9) + consider a longer recovery block (2+ weeks) before resuming high volume
Re-audit nutrition/sleep/stress before further programming changes — even advanced lifters most often plateau from recovery/nutrition gaps, not programming flaws

rows.Problem: Specialization block not producing visible results
rows.Fix: Check that non-specialized muscles were genuinely dropped to MEV (common error: not reducing other volume enough, causing systemic overreach)
Insert a full deload or even a 1-2 week "active recovery" block; advanced lifters are as vulnerable to psychological burnout as physical overreach

rows.Best Use: Time efficiency, minimal added fatigue since muscles are independent
Adds significant fatigue; use sparingly, end of session

rows.Structure: 4+ exercises in sequence, often same muscle group
rows.Best Use: High fatigue; advanced only; typically for a single "finisher" muscle group at end of session

rows.Max Intensity-Technique \"Instances\"/Mu: 2-4 (only if volume elsewhere is correspondingly reduced)

rows.Calories: Maintenance/slight surplus in off-season, maintenance near meet
-300 to -750 kcal (larger deficits for higher body fat, smaller near goal)

rows.Adjustment: Standard tables apply; typically tolerates larger surpluses without proportionally more fat gain due to generally higher muscle-building ceiling relative to fat cell response, individual variation still primary factor

Often benefits from slightly smaller deficits/surpluses proportionally (relative to bodyweight) and slightly higher relative protein intake target during fat loss; menstrual cycle can affect hunger/water retention — track trends over weeks, not single days

Favor lean bulk/maintenance over aggressive cutting; avoid deficits during active growth years unless medically indicated; emphasize skill/technique and general fitness over aggressive body-composition goals

Protein target toward the higher end of ranges (helps offset age-related anabolic resistance); slightly more conservative deficit sizing to protect muscle mass and hormonal health; recomposition remains viable longer in previously untrained or detrained older clients

rows.Fix: Audit actual intake (frequently underestimated); increase calories 10-15% and reassess in 2 weeks
Audit tracking accuracy, water retention/stress, and reassess deficit size; increase activity/cardio before crashing calories further
Deficit too aggressive or volume cut too much — rebalance: protect resistance training, adjust cardio/calories instead

rows.Problem: Client feels their recomposition isn't visibly progressing
rows.Fix: Set expectations early: recomp is genuinely slower than sequential bulk/cut and best tracked via measurements/photos over 8-12 week windows, not weekly scale weight

rows.Recommended Frequency per Muscle: 2x/week (standard; supports MAV distribution without excess per-session volume)
1.6–2x/week (2x standard on most splits; 1x acceptable ONLY with high per-session volume in specialization contexts, e.g., bro split)

rows.Volume Adjustment from Base Table: Reduce accessory/isolation volume ~30-40%; keep compound volume, since heavy compounds are the primary driver
Maintain near-current MEV-MAV; do not deliberately increase volume during a cut (recovery capacity is reduced by the deficit)
Reduce isolation volume in favor of compound/power work; total set count can be lower since intensity/specificity matters more
MEV is sufficient; no need to chase MAV

rows.Adjustment: Reduce planned volume 15-25%
Reduce planned volume 15-25%

rows.Factor: Excellent recovery (8h+ sleep, low stress, good nutrition)
rows.Adjustment: Can push toward the upper MAV/MRV range
Start at MEV, ramp to MAV over 2-3 weeks

rows.Factor: Approaching a deload (week 5-8 of a block)
rows.Adjustment: Volume naturally at its highest planned point (MAV-MRV) right before cutting back

rows.Note: Often tolerates the upper end of MAV, especially for lower body (quads/glutes/hamstrings), sooner in training career than typical male trend data — adjust upward if recovery markers support it
Use beginner/intermediate ranges regardless of enthusiasm; prioritize technique over volume-chasing
Can use standard intermediate/advanced tables but should monitor joint feedback more closely at the upper MAV/MRV end

Consider capping at MAV rather than pushing MRV even if "advanced," due to slower connective tissue recovery; frequency (2x/week) becomes even more valuable relative to raw volume for managing per-session fatigue

rows.Fix: Cut volume 30-40% for that muscle for 1-2 weeks

rows.Symptom: No progress despite good recovery markers
rows.Fix: Increase volume 20-30% and reassess in 2-3 weeks

rows.Symptom: Joint pain localized to one muscle's training
rows.Likely Volume Issue: Could be volume OR exercise selection
rows.Fix: Check file 9 for exercise substitution before assuming volume is the sole issue

rows.Likely Volume Issue: Global volume too high relative to recovery capacity (life stress, sleep)
rows.Fix: Reduce volume across the board or insert a deload (file 10)

rows.Detail: Resistance training before cardio when both goals matter; cardio-first only if cardio is the primary goal or it's a dedicated warm-up (≤10 min Zone 1)
Separate high-intensity cardio and heavy lower-body lifting by ≥6 hours where schedule allows; if not possible, prioritize the primary goal's session quality
For hypertrophy/strength-primary clients, place HIIT/hard cardio on non-leg-heavy days or as separate low-impact Zone 2
Total weekly cardio duration should not exceed 150-200% of resistance training duration for clients with hypertrophy/strength as primary goal (protects recovery capacity)
If fat loss is primary but client also wants strength gains, cap cardio HIIT to 1x/week, favor Zone 2 for remaining sessions to reduce systemic fatigue

rows.Substitution Logic: Bodyweight circuits (burpees, mountain climbers, jump squats), walking/jogging outdoors
Hill sprints, stair repeats, distance walking/running, bodyweight circuits

rows.Preferred Modality: Swimming, cycling (low resistance), rowing (pain-free ROM only)

rows.Avoid: Rowing with poor form, high-impact jumping

rows.Cardio Adjustment: Full prescription, HIIT permitted if otherwise eligible
Zone 1-2 only, reduce total duration 30-40%, no intervals
Cardio duration capped at 50% normal, Zone 1-2 only

rows.Symptom: Strength stalling despite good lifting adherence
rows.Fix: Apply Section 4 volume ceiling, shift cardio to Zone 2

rows.Symptom: Client reports high fatigue despite following prescription
rows.Likely Cause: Recovery score not queried before cardio prescription
rows.Fix: Enforce Section 9 gate before finalizing weekly cardio plan

rows.Symptom: Fat loss stalled despite cardio adherence
rows.Likely Cause: Cardio alone insufficient; check nutrition adherence (file 11 check-in)
rows.Fix: Cross-reference `adherence_nutrition_pct`, address diet before adding more cardio

rows.Value: 2–3 (never more, regardless of client request, until unlocked)
Full body, same routine each session (no undulation)
Bodyweight or light-to-moderate load only; RPE cap 6/10
None (fixed template) until re-screened and tier upgraded
None permitted; Zone 1-2 walking only, 15-20 min optional

rows.Exercise: Incline push-up (hands elevated) or wall push-up
Band row or supported dumbbell row (light)

rows.Notes: Must stay within tier's hard caps unless Section 2.3 applies
Cannot be overridden by anyone in-app; only a documented physician clearance changes downstream routing, and only after the acute event is resolved

rows.Overridable by Certified Coach?: No, cannot be cleared by trainer
rows.Notes: Only physician-sourced clearance input clears this; a coach can still choose to work with the client off-system under their own professional liability, but the app will not represent this client as cleared

rows.Trigger: Age corrected/verified ≥13 with guardian consent, or referral resolved
8 consecutive weeks consistent check-ins + full intake completeness ≥95% + no unresolved flags
Missed check-in cycle, or new minor flag
New high-risk condition disclosed, new unresolved pain flag, 2 missed check-in cycles

rows.Rule: All override attempts blocked; system logs attempt and notifies gym admin to complete verification

rows.Edge Case: Two coaches submit conflicting overrides same day
rows.Rule: Most recent timestamped override is active; both remain in audit trail
Confidence tier and flags transfer with client; override history remains visible to new gym's admin but override authority does not retroactively apply
Historical overrides remain in audit trail (immutable); no new overrides possible from deactivated account

rows.Symptom: Client stuck in DEFAULT_SAFE_TEMPLATE despite seeming fine
rows.Cause: Exit criteria fail-closed default with no resolved condition
rows.Fix: Verify which Section 1.5 branch applies; likely missing movement screen or unresolved flag

rows.Symptom: Coach override not taking effect in generated program
rows.Cause: Override not passed into generation pipeline context
rows.Fix: Ensure override schema object is merged into `client_state` before program generation call
